message.abstract: <jats:title>Ancient genomics of horse domestication</jats:title><jats:p>The domestication of the horse was a seminal event in human cultural evolution. Librado<jats:italic>et al.</jats:italic>obtained genome sequences from 14 horses from the Bronze and Iron Ages, about 2000 to 4000 years ago, soon after domestication. They identified variants determining coat color and genes selected during the domestication process. They could also see evidence of admixture with archaic horses and the demography of the domestication process, which included the accumulation of deleterious variants. The horse appears to have undergone a different type of domestication process than animals that were domesticated simply for food.</jats:p><jats:p><jats:italic>Science</jats:italic>, this issue p.<jats:related-article xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink" ext-link-type="doi" issue="6336" page="442" related-article-type="in-this-issue" vol="356" xlink:href="10.1126/science.aam5298">442</jats:related-article></jats:p>
title: Ancient genomic changes associated with domestication of the horse
